title: A Device Hidden in Cars Across the US Leaves Them Vulnerable to Hacking and Paralysis. Patch It Now
gist: UCSD researchers found the Bluetooth KARR Security System aftermarket car alarm, installed by dealers in over 2 million US vehicles, shares a single universal authentication key that lets any nearby hacker unlock, track, or paralyze the car; vendor Acrisure took 18 months to patch a flaw car owners often don't know exists.

insights.claim: The KARR Security System aftermarket car alarm uses a single authentication key shared across all devices, extractable from the KARR smartphone app, letting a hacker spoof radio commands accepted by any nearby Bluetooth-enabled KARR unit.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: A shared-key architecture means one reverse-engineered app compromises every device deployed, an existential product-liability exposure for the vendor.
insights.quote: unlock “every single car they've ever put this in.”
insights.locator: The security flaw that makes all of those sabotage

insights.claim: The KARR flaw lets a hacker within Bluetooth range silently unlock a car, disable its alarm, or disable the ignition to strand the driver, though it cannot start the ignition itself.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Combined with a cheap online locksmith tool, it enables stealthy theft without breaking a window, raising theft-loss and insurance implications.
insights.locator: That's the disturbing discovery of a team

insights.claim: Even in a 'deactivated' state for owners who declined to pay, the KARR device keeps beaconing and accepting Bluetooth signals whenever the car is on and for up to 10 minutes after shutoff, and can be remotely reactivated into a hackable state.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Non-paying owners get no meaningful warning, widening the attack surface beyond active customers.
insights.locator: The KARR device's insecurity is compounded

insights.claim: UCSD estimates more than 2 million Bluetooth-enabled KARR devices have been deployed in US vehicles, extrapolated from WiGLE crowdsourced radio scans and device serial numbers.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Scale plus owner ignorance makes remediation nearly impossible, a template for aftermarket IoT liability risk.
insights.locator: To get a count of how many vulnerable KARR devices

insights.claim: KARR alarms are installed by car dealers to deter lot theft and typically remain wired into the vehicle even when buyers decline the feature, so owners possess a hackable device they never purchased or knew about.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: The dealer-installed distribution model severs owners from the patch supply chain, unlike manufacturer-issued recalls.
insights.locator: The KARR alarm devices are typically installed

insights.claim: A security expert who co-led the first car-hacking research in 2010-2011 called KARR 'probably the worst' car hacking threat ever discovered because the manufacturer can't fix it and owners don't know they have the problem.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Frames aftermarket components as the emerging weak link in vehicle cybersecurity, distinct from OEM software.
insights.quote: It provides all the elements a car thief would want, but you have none of the advantages we normally have in terms of defending it, because you're disconnected from the supply chain that put it there.
insights.locator: That difficulty of getting word out

insights.claim: The KARR device's persistent Bluetooth signature lets attackers use the WiGLE database to track a vulnerable car's historical locations and identify where it is frequently parked, a scouting mechanism for targeted theft or sabotage.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Turns a measurement tool into a surveillance vector, broadening the harm beyond opportunistic hacking.
insights.locator: Those WiGLE results are, however, more than just

insights.claim: In one 20-minute drive around UCSD's campus, researchers scanning with an ordinary Android phone found 97 vehicles with beaconing KARR alarms, illustrating pervasiveness.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Demonstrates trivial discoverability with no special equipment, raising real-world exploit likelihood.
insights.locator: In the UCSD team’s own scanning

insights.claim: Acrisure Protection Group took close to 18 months to ship a firmware patch after UCSD disclosed the flaw in January of last year, releasing it only weeks before planned Defcon and Usenix presentations.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Slow patch cadence despite 'prompt' claims signals weak security governance at the vendor and its SWDS subsidiary.

insights.claim: Acrisure characterized the vulnerability as highly complex and low risk under real-world conditions, a claim WIRED's demonstrated 'mayhem' exploits triggering horns and lights across multiple cars directly contradict.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Vendor risk-minimization language is a red flag when independent demos show one-tap mass exploitation.
insights.quote: The vulnerability described in [UCSD's] research is highly complex and presents a low risk to customers under real-world conditions.
insights.locator: When WIRED reached out to Acrisure

insights.claim: The patch requires owners to install the KARR Security app, connect to their vehicle, and manually trigger a firmware update, meaning remediation depends on owners first discovering the hidden device via a window sticker or dashboard button.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Manual, owner-initiated patching guarantees low remediation rates across resold and unaware-owner vehicles.
insights.locator: Car owners who already have the KARR Security smartphone app
